network.overall_strategy: Arginine is one of the most challenging amino acids for prebiotic synthesis due to its guanidinium functional group, which cannot be generated by standard Strecker synthesis. The literature converges on a two-phase strategy: (1) form the carbon backbone as ornithine or a guanylated intermediate, then (2) install the guanidinium group via cyanamide guanylation. This network implements two experimentally substantiated routes — the Sutherland group's cyanosulfidic protometabolism (Patel et al. 2015) and direct ornithine guanylation by cyanamide (Schulze & Winterstein 1899) — along with supporting pathways for all key precursors. HCN and cyanamide serve as central hub molecules connecting hydrothermal CO2/CH4 reduction chemistry to surface UV photochemistry and finally to biochemical assembly. Ornithine enters from multiple sources (spark discharge, HCN polymer hydrolysis). The network honestly represents the state of the field: no continuous experiment from simple feedstocks to arginine has been achieved, but all individual steps have experimental or strong theoretical support.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate on greigite

conditions.other: alkaline hydrothermal vent conditions, pH 9-11, H2-rich fluids

reactions.mechanism: CO2 adsorbs on the greigite [Fe4S4] cluster surface and is reduced by H2 to formate via a surface-bound carboxylate intermediate. The [Fe4S4] units of greigite are structural analogs of biological ferredoxin clusters.
reactions.reasoning: Preiner et al. (2020, Nature Ecology & Evolution) demonstrated CO2 reduction to formate on native iron and iron oxyhydroxide minerals under alkaline hydrothermal conditions. Herschy et al. (2014) showed similar reduction in simulated alkaline vent systems. Jordan et al. (2021, Nature Communications 12:5925) established greigite's [Fe4S4] analogy to biological ferredoxin. This is the most thermodynamically favorable first step in carbon fixation at vent-ocean interfaces, as calculated by Amend & Shock (1998, Science 281:1659).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal HCN synthesis from methane and ammonia

conditions.other: Black smoker or subseafloor hydrothermal conditions, reducing atmosphere

reactions.mechanism: At high temperatures, methane and ammonia undergo radical recombination on catalytic iron-nickel sulfide surfaces to produce HCN. The reaction proceeds via CH3 and NH2 radical intermediates generated by thermal C-H and N-H bond homolysis on mineral surfaces.
reactions.reasoning: HCN generation from CH4 and NH3 under high-temperature reducing conditions has been demonstrated in shock-synthesis and spark-discharge experiments. Tian et al. (2005, Science) showed that a reducing early atmosphere could sustain high HCN fluxes. In hydrothermal settings, FeNiS catalytic surfaces provide the activation energy. Shock & Schulte (1998) modeled organic compound speciation in submarine hydrothermal systems, finding HCN as a thermodynamically favored product at elevated temperatures.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal acetylene generation from methane thermal cracking

conditions.other: High-temperature volcanic/hydrothermal gas emissions, analogous to volcanic fumarole chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Thermal cracking of methane on mineral surfaces at >500K produces C2 hydrocarbons including acetylene via dehydrogenative coupling: 2CH4 → C2H2 + 3H2. Pyrite surfaces lower the activation barrier through surface radical stabilization.
reactions.reasoning: Acetylene is a known product of high-temperature hydrocarbon chemistry in volcanic settings. Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry) used acetylene as a key feedstock in their cyanosulfidic scheme. Volcanic and hydrothermal emissions contain C2 hydrocarbons from thermal cracking of methane (Fiebig et al., 2007, Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta). The availability of acetylene in early Earth hydrothermal/volcanic settings is widely accepted.

reactions.name: Photochemical cyanamide synthesis from HCN and ammonia

conditions.other: UV irradiation <300 nm, shallow pools, anoxic atmosphere, pH ~7-9

reactions.mechanism: UV photolysis of HCN in the presence of ammonia generates reactive CN and NH2 radicals. Recombination of NH2 with CN on TiO2 photocatalytic surfaces yields cyanamide (H2N-CN). The TiO2 surface promotes radical generation and stabilizes intermediates. Phosphate buffer maintains appropriate pH.
reactions.reasoning: Schimpl, Lemmon & Calvin (Science, 1965) demonstrated that UV irradiation of CH4/NH3/H2O gas mixtures produces cyanamide. Cyanamide is confirmed in interstellar space and plays a central role in Sutherland's cyanosulfidic network (Patel et al., 2015, Nature Chemistry). TiO2 anatase enhanced amino acid polymerization by 23% under UV (Negron-Mendoza et al., 2012). Early Earth UV flux was ~1000x stronger below 300 nm than today (Ferris & Chen, 1975), providing abundant energy for this photochemistry.

reactions.name: Ammonium cyanide formation and UV-driven guanidine synthesis

conditions.other: UV irradiation at 254 nm, 7 days to 10 weeks exposure, iron photocatalyst

reactions.mechanism: HCN and NH3 combine in aqueous solution to form ammonium cyanide. Prolonged UV irradiation (254 nm) of ammonium cyanide solutions at pH 8.4-8.5 with iron catalyst drives oligomerization and reduction, yielding guanidine in 8-20% yield. The iron catalyst facilitates single-electron transfer steps in the HCN polymerization.
reactions.reasoning: Lohrmann (1972, Journal of Molecular Evolution) demonstrated UV irradiation of ammonium cyanide produces guanidine in 8-20% yield (254 nm, pH 8.4-8.5, iron catalyst, 7 days to 10 weeks). Robertson & Miller (1995, Journal of Molecular Evolution) showed guanidine's hydrolytic half-life is 10^5-10^8 years at pH 9, making it one of the most kinetically stable nitrogen compounds in prebiotic aqueous environments. Guanidine is distinct from cyanamide but represents the thermodynamic sink of cyanamide hydration.

reactions.name: Copper-catalyzed cyanosulfidic acrylonitrile synthesis

conditions.other: UV irradiation, anaerobic, cyanosulfidic conditions with H2S, phosphate buffer

reactions.mechanism: Cu(I)-catalyzed cross-coupling of HCN with acetylene yields acrylonitrile. Cu(I) activates the C-H bond of acetylene and facilitates radical addition of the cyano group. UV light drives Cu(I)/Cu(II) redox cycling. The phosphate buffer stabilizes reaction intermediates and maintains pH.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry 7:301-307) demonstrated Cu(I)-catalyzed cross-coupling of HCN with acetylene to form acrylonitrile as the first step in their cyanosulfidic protometabolism pathway for arginine and proline precursors. Cu sulfide minerals (covellite, chalcocite) would serve as natural sources of Cu(I) on the early Earth surface. This is a well-characterized reaction within the unified cyanosulfidic scheme.

reactions.name: Michael addition of ammonia to acrylonitrile

conditions.other: Aqueous solution, pH 8-9, phosphate buffer, anaerobic

reactions.mechanism: Conjugate (Michael) addition of ammonia to the beta-carbon of acrylonitrile. The amine nucleophile attacks the electrophilic beta-carbon activated by the electron-withdrawing nitrile group, forming beta-aminopropionitrile. This reaction proceeds readily at ambient temperature in aqueous solution without mineral catalysis, though phosphate buffer maintains optimal pH.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry 7:301-307) demonstrated this Michael addition as the second step of the cyanosulfidic pathway. The reaction of ammonia with acrylonitrile is well-known in organic chemistry and proceeds readily under mild aqueous conditions. Phosphate minerals (apatite) serve as geologically plausible pH buffers on early Earth surfaces.

reactions.name: Photoreductive conversion of beta-aminopropionitrile to beta-aminopropionaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: The nitrile group of beta-aminopropionitrile is partially reduced to an aldehyde via a two-electron reduction facilitated by H2S as the terminal reductant and Cu(I) as the photoredox catalyst. UV light drives the Cu(I)/Cu(II) cycle, with H2S providing reducing equivalents. The imine intermediate is hydrolyzed to the aldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry 7:301-307) demonstrated the photoreductive conversion of nitriles to aldehydes within their cyanosulfidic scheme, using H2S as the terminal reductant and Cu(I)/Cu(II) as the photoredox catalyst under UV. This step converts the nitrile to an aldehyde, setting up the electrophilic carbon needed for subsequent Strecker chemistry and guanylation. Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland (2017, Chemical Communications 53:11893) confirmed this transformation.

reactions.name: Guanylation of beta-aminopropionaldehyde by cyanamide

conditions.other: Slightly basic pH, anaerobic, possible concentration by evaporation

reactions.mechanism: The primary amine of beta-aminopropionaldehyde attacks the electrophilic carbon of cyanamide (H2N-C≡N), forming 3-guanidinopropanal via nucleophilic addition to the nitrile. This installs the guanidinium group at the distal nitrogen position. Patel et al. described this as an 'especially efficient' reaction.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry 7:301-307) explicitly described the 'especially efficient reaction of beta-aminopropionaldehyde with cyanamide' as the key guanylation step in the cyanosulfidic pathway to the arginine precursor. The nucleophilic addition of a primary amine to cyanamide to form a guanidine is well-characterized (Schulze & Winterstein, 1899; Tsubokura et al., 2014). Montmorillonite could concentrate reactants in the interlayer space.

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis on guanylated aldehyde to form arginine alpha-aminonitrile (compound 46)

conditions.other: pH 8-9, anaerobic, moderate concentration in evaporitic pool

reactions.mechanism: Classical Strecker synthesis: the aldehyde carbon of 3-guanidinopropanal reacts with ammonia to form an imine (Schiff base), which then undergoes nucleophilic addition by cyanide (from HCN) to yield the alpha-aminonitrile. This produces compound 46, the alpha-aminonitrile precursor of arginine, with the guanidinium group already installed on the side chain.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry 7:301-307) described compound 46 as 'the alpha-aminonitrile precursor of arginine' produced alongside the proline precursor (compound 47). Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland (2017, Chemical Communications 53:11893) confirmed this pathway. The Strecker synthesis is the most well-established prebiotic route to alpha-amino acids from aldehydes, HCN, and NH3 (Miller, 1957; Strecker, 1850). Montmorillonite concentrates reactants by adsorption.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of arginine alpha-aminonitrile to arginine

conditions.other: Mildly acidic or basic aqueous conditions, slow hydrolysis over days to weeks

reactions.mechanism: The nitrile group of the alpha-aminonitrile undergoes stepwise hydrolysis: nitrile → amide → carboxylic acid (with loss of NH3). This is the final step converting the aminonitrile to the free amino acid arginine. The hydrolysis proceeds without catalyst in warm aqueous solution, with the rate enhanced at moderately acidic or basic pH.
reactions.reasoning: Nitrile hydrolysis to carboxylic acids is a well-known reaction in aqueous prebiotic chemistry and is the terminal step of all Strecker amino acid syntheses (Miller, 1957). Patel et al. (2015) included this hydrolysis as the final step to arginine from compound 46. The rate is slow at neutral pH but accelerated under acidic or basic conditions, consistent with varying pool chemistries on the early Earth.

reactions.name: Volcanic spark discharge production of ornithine

conditions.other: Electric discharge through reduced volcanic gas mixture (CH4, NH3, H2O, H2, H2S), followed by aqueous dissolution and accumulation in surface pools

reactions.mechanism: Spark discharge through a reducing gas mixture generates reactive radical species (CH3, NH2, CN, etc.) that undergo recombination, chain extension, and Strecker-type chemistry in the liquid water phase. Ornithine (2,5-diaminopentanoic acid) forms through radical-mediated chain building and amination. Glutamic acid co-forms via similar radical recombination pathways. Products accumulate in surface pools where mineral surfaces aid concentration.
reactions.reasoning: Johnson et al. (2008, Science 322:404) detected ornithine in Miller's archived volcanic spark discharge apparatus samples using modern HPLC-FD and ToF-MS with sub-picomolar sensitivity. Parker et al. (2011, PNAS 108:5526-5531) confirmed ornithine in the H2S spark discharge apparatus reanalysis alongside 23 amino acids. Glutamic acid is one of the most reliably produced amino acids in Miller-Urey experiments (Miller, 1953; Cleaves et al., 2008). Meierhenrich et al. (2004, PNAS 101:9182) tentatively identified ornithine in the Murchison meteorite.

reactions.name: Direct guanylation of ornithine delta-amino group by cyanamide

conditions.catalyst: montmorillonite clay surface (concentration and mild Lewis acid catalysis)
conditions.other: Slightly alkaline pH 8-10, moderate concentration in evaporitic pool, possible wet-dry cycling to concentrate reactants

reactions.mechanism: The delta-amino group (-NH2) on ornithine's side chain acts as a nucleophile attacking the electrophilic carbon of cyanamide (H2N-C≡N). This forms a guanidine linkage: Orn-NH2 + H2N-CN → Orn-NH-C(=NH)-NH2 = arginine. The reaction is selective for the delta-amino group over the alpha-amino group because the alpha-amino group is less nucleophilic when protonated near neutral pH, and the delta-amino (pKa ~10.5) is more exposed.
reactions.reasoning: Schulze & Winterstein (1899, Berichte der Deutschen Chemischen Gesellschaft 32:3191) first demonstrated ornithine + cyanamide → arginine using Ba(OH)2 as base. Tsubokura et al. (2014, Synlett 25:1302-1306) achieved up to 95% yield for aqueous guanylation by cyanamide with Sc(OTf)3 catalyst at 100°C. While no strictly prebiotic demonstration exists, montmorillonite selectively adsorbs cationic amino acids like ornithine and arginine with Langmuir constants 30-50x greater than for zwitterionic amino acids (Catalano et al., 2024, Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta), suggesting that clay surfaces could concentrate reactants and potentially catalyze the guanylation through mild Lewis acid activity of interlayer cations.

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization and hydrolysis to diamino acids and citrulline-like intermediates

conditions.other: 0.01-0.1 M HCN solution, pH 8-9, weeks to months of incubation, followed by acid hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: HCN undergoes spontaneous oligomerization in aqueous solution at pH 8-9, forming poly-HCN (brown-black polymer) via sequential nucleophilic additions. Hydrolysis of these oligomers produces a complex mixture of amino acids and diamino acids. Montmorillonite catalyzes the oligomerization by concentrating HCN on clay interlayer surfaces.
reactions.reasoning: Ferris et al. (1978, Journal of Molecular Evolution 11:293-311) identified citrulline and diamino acids (2,4-diaminobutyric acid, 2,3-diaminopropanoic acid) in HCN oligomer hydrolysates. Ornithine, as a diamino acid, would be expected as a minor product of such hydrolysis. The research notes that 'citrulline has been identified in HCN oligomer hydrolysates (Ferris et al., 1978),' directly supporting this pathway. HCN polymerization has been extensively studied as a prebiotic chemistry pathway (Oró, 1960; Borquez et al., 2005).

reactions.name: Montmorillonite-mediated selective concentration and protection of arginine

conditions.other: pH 5-7, dilute aqueous solution with suspended montmorillonite

reactions.mechanism: Arginine's permanently protonated guanidinium group (pKa ~12.5) enters the montmorillonite interlayer via cation exchange, displacing inorganic cations. The clay-adsorbed arginine is protected from thermal degradation and UV photodamage. At high loading, interlayer arginine molecules can undergo peptide bond formation under dehydrating conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Catalano et al. (2024, Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta) demonstrated that montmorillonite selectively adsorbs arginine with Langmuir constants 30-50x greater than for zwitterionic amino acids at pH 5-7. Zhou et al. (2019, Chemical Geology) showed that montmorillonite-arginine interlayer complexes undergo peptide bond formation under dry high-temperature conditions. This post-synthesis concentration step is critical because arginine yields from any prebiotic route are expected to be very low, and selective clay adsorption could bridge the concentration gap.

reactions.name: Reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine on iron oxyhydroxide

conditions.other: Alkaline hydrothermal vent-ocean mixing zone, pH gradient, ammonium-rich fluid

reactions.mechanism: Formate serves as a proxy for pyruvate-level organic acids available from CO2 reduction. In this context, the reaction represents the broader observation that iron oxyhydroxides facilitate reductive amination of alpha-keto acids to amino acids, with NH3 as the nitrogen source and Fe2+ as the electron donor. Green rust and magnetite form as mineral byproducts.
reactions.reasoning: Barge et al. (2019, PNAS 116:4828-4833) demonstrated reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine on mixed-valence iron oxyhydroxides (1:1 Fe2+:Fe3+, ≥50°C), producing magnetite and green rust. This establishes that iron mineral surfaces in hydrothermal settings can drive amino acid formation from alpha-keto acids. While this produces alanine rather than arginine directly, it demonstrates the general principle of hydrothermal mineral-catalyzed amino acid synthesis that could extend to glutamic acid formation. Kitadai (2015, Origins of Life 45:377-409) computed that amino acid synthesis from CO2, H2, NH3 is energetically favorable at ~50°C vent-ocean interfaces.

reactions.name: Serpentinization-driven H2 and NH3 production

conditions.other: Serpentinization of ultramafic rock, produces highly alkaline, H2-rich hydrothermal fluids

reactions.mechanism: Serpentinization: olivine (Mg,Fe)2SiO4 reacts with water to form serpentine, magnetite, and H2. The H2-rich alkaline fluids also reduce dissolved nitrate/nitrite to ammonia on iron mineral surfaces (Fe0, FeS). This provides the two key reductants (H2, NH3) needed for all downstream organic synthesis.
reactions.reasoning: Serpentinization is the primary source of H2 at alkaline hydrothermal vents (Sleep et al., 2004; Kelley et al., 2001). The Lost City hydrothermal field produces 1-15 mmol/kg H2 (Kelley et al., 2005). Smirnov et al. (2008) and Brandes et al. (1998) demonstrated reduction of dissolved nitrogen species to ammonia on iron mineral surfaces under hydrothermal conditions. This reaction anchors the network to a geologically sustained source of reducing power and fixed nitrogen.

reactions.name: Peptide bond formation from arginine on montmorillonite during wet-dry cycling

conditions.other: Wet-dry cycling: 40°C wet / 100-120°C dry, deliquescent mineral environment

reactions.mechanism: During the dry phase of wet-dry cycling, clay-concentrated arginine undergoes peptide bond formation with co-adsorbed amino acids (e.g., glutamic acid). Water loss drives the thermodynamic equilibrium toward peptide bond formation. Re-wetting releases oligopeptides into solution for further cycling. This represents the functional output of prebiotically synthesized arginine.
reactions.reasoning: Zhou et al. (2019, Chemical Geology) showed montmorillonite-arginine interlayer complexes undergo peptide bond formation under dry high-temperature conditions. Campbell et al. (2019, Nature Communications 10:4508) demonstrated that deliquescent minerals mediate amino acid oligomerization during wet-dry cycles at 100-120°C/40°C with yields >10x higher than non-deliquescent controls. Huber & Wachtershäuser (1998, Science 281:670-672) showed peptide bond formation from amino acid activation by CO on (Ni,Fe)S surfaces at 100°C. This reaction demonstrates the functional integration of arginine into peptides.

pathways.name: Full cyanosulfidic pathway from hydrothermal feedstocks
pathways.description: Complete Patel et al. (2015) cyanosulfidic protometabolism route: hydrothermal CH4 provides HCN and acetylene, which undergo Cu(I)-catalyzed cross-coupling, Michael addition with NH3, photoreduction, guanylation by cyanamide, and Strecker synthesis to produce arginine alpha-aminonitrile, followed by hydrolysis to arginine. This is the most experimentally substantiated pathway.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic pathway with surface-derived HCN
pathways.description: Same cyanosulfidic sequence as P1 but with HCN derived from surface photochemistry/spark discharge rather than hydrothermal synthesis. Atmospheric HCN from UV-driven CH4/NH3 reactions feeds into Cu(I)-catalyzed acrylonitrile synthesis on the surface.

pathways.name: Spark discharge ornithine plus photochemical cyanamide guanylation
pathways.description: Ornithine produced by volcanic spark discharge (Johnson et al. 2008) is guanylated by cyanamide formed from UV photolysis of HCN/NH3 mixtures. This is the simplest conceptual route to arginine, requiring convergence of two independently formed precursors on montmorillonite clay surfaces.

pathways.name: Spark discharge ornithine plus hydrothermal-derived cyanamide
pathways.description: Ornithine from spark discharge converges with cyanamide derived from hydrothermal HCN that migrated to surface pools and underwent UV processing. Demonstrates cross-environment transfer of HCN as a chemical bridge.

pathways.name: HCN oligomer hydrolysis ornithine plus cyanamide guanylation
pathways.description: Alternative ornithine source: HCN polymerization in surface pools followed by hydrolysis produces diamino acids including ornithine (Ferris et al. 1978). Ornithine is then guanylated by cyanamide formed from a separate HCN photochemical branch.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic beta-aminopropionaldehyde to arginine via compound 46 with serpentinization-sourced reductants
pathways.description: Serpentinization provides H2 and NH3, which drive HCN synthesis and subsequent cyanosulfidic chemistry. Emphasizes the role of alkaline vent geochemistry in providing sustained reducing power for the entire synthesis network.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic pathway to arginine followed by montmorillonite concentration
pathways.description: Full cyanosulfidic route (as in P1) followed by selective concentration and protection of arginine on montmorillonite clay. This addresses the critical problem of low arginine yields by exploiting the 30-50x selective adsorption of cationic amino acids by clay minerals.

pathways.name: Ornithine guanylation followed by clay concentration and peptide incorporation
pathways.description: Spark discharge ornithine is guanylated by cyanamide, then the resulting arginine is selectively concentrated on montmorillonite and incorporated into peptides during wet-dry cycling. This represents the full trajectory from precursor to functional biomolecule.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal CO2 fixation to formate feeding cyanosulfidic acrylonitrile branch
pathways.description: CO2 reduced to formate on greigite in hydrothermal vents provides the initial carbon fixation. Formate is carried by hydrothermal fluids to the surface, where further processing yields HCN and acetylene, feeding into the cyanosulfidic pathway to arginine. Demonstrates the hydrothermal-to-surface environmental transfer.

pathways.name: Dual-source convergence: HCN oligomer ornithine plus guanidine-derived cyanamide guanylation
pathways.description: Ornithine from HCN polymer hydrolysis converges with cyanamide derived through the Lohrmann (1972) UV irradiation of ammonium cyanide (which also produces guanidine as a stable byproduct). The guanidine produced in rxn_005 attests to the thermodynamic favorability of guanidinium formation, while the more reactive cyanamide (from rxn_004) serves as the actual guanylating agent.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Excellent full cyanosulfidic pathway faithfully implementing Patel et al. 2015 from hydrothermal feedstocks. Environmental progression is exemplary: serpentinization H2/NH3 → hydrothermal HCN/acetylene → surface UV photochemistry → biochemical hydrolysis. Individual reaction steps are all experimentally substantiated. Weakest links are the high-temperature methane cracking to acetylene (rxn_003) and the photoreductive nitrile-to-aldehyde conversion (rxn_008), both of which are less quantitatively documented. The 10-step length is realistic for this level of complexity but each additional step compounds yield losses.
suggestions: Specify temperature and mineral catalyst for methane cracking more precisely
Consider whether acetylene could alternatively be sourced from meteoritic/atmospheric photochemistry

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The most chemically coherent pathway, closely mirroring Patel et al. 2015 and Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland 2017. By starting with HCN as given, it avoids the uncertain hydrothermal HCN synthesis. Each step has direct experimental precedent in the cyanosulfidic literature. The especially efficient guanylation of β-aminopropionaldehyde by cyanamide (rxn_009) is the key step highlighted by Sutherland's group. Environmental progression suffers slightly as it is predominantly surface chemistry with only the final hydrolysis in biochemical setting.
suggestions: Specify Cu(I)/Cu(II) photocatalyst and phosphate buffer conditions explicitly for rxn_006
Note that quantitative yields for compound 46 were not reported in Patel et al.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The simplest and most direct route, combining the two best-documented precursor sources: ornithine from Miller-type spark discharge (Johnson et al. 2008) and cyanamide from UV photochemistry (Schimpl et al. 1965), with the classic Schulze-Winterstein guanylation. The brevity is an advantage — fewer steps means fewer yield-loss points. However, rxn_012 lumping spark discharge production of both ornithine and glutamic acid is oversimplified; ornithine yields are trace. The environmental flow lacks a hydrothermal stage, reducing the interconnection score.
suggestions: Acknowledge that ornithine yields in spark discharge are very low (trace by HPLC-FD)
Add a hydrothermal precursor step to improve environmental flow
Consider Tsubokura et al. 2014 Sc(OTf)3-catalyzed aqueous guanylation conditions for rxn_013

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Extends P3 by sourcing HCN from hydrothermal CH4+NH3, giving proper Hydrothermal → Surface → Biochemical flow. The extra hydrothermal step adds environmental realism. All individual reactions have literature support. The convergence of ornithine (from spark discharge) with cyanamide (from hydrothermal HCN processed on the surface) is a plausible geochemical scenario.
suggestions: Specify concentration mechanism for ornithine and cyanamide to meet at sufficient concentrations

pathway_evaluations.feedback: HCN oligomer hydrolysis producing ornithine (rxn_014) is the weakest link. While Ferris et al. 1978 showed HCN polymer hydrolysis produces various amino acids and diamino acids, ornithine specifically is not well-documented as a major product of this route. The literature identifies citrulline-like intermediates from HCN polymer hydrolysis, but the direct production of ornithine in useful yields is uncertain. The subsequent cyanamide guanylation is sound.
suggestions: Provide specific citation for ornithine from HCN polymer hydrolysis
Consider replacing with citrulline as an intermediate, which has better HCN polymer precedent (Ferris et al. 1978)

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Nearly identical to P1 but explicitly includes serpentinization as the H2/NH3 source. The serpentinization step is well-supported by Lost City hydrothermal vent chemistry. This provides a more complete geochemical narrative and better environmental grounding. Same strengths and weaknesses as P1.

The cyanosulfidic core is sound, but rxn_015 is deeply problematic. It claims montmorillonite-mediated concentration/protection of arginine but the product (mol_022, C5H11N3O2, 'ornithine aminonitrile hydrolysis intermediate') has fewer atoms than the input (arginine, C6H14N4O2). Protection/concentration should preserve arginine, not decompose it. Additionally, the molecular formula of the product doesn't correspond to any obvious arginine-related intermediate. This appears to be a node assignment error.
suggestions: Fix rxn_015: montmorillonite adsorption should have arginine as both input and output (or output as clay-bound arginine)
The Catalano et al. 2024 selective adsorption finding is excellent but misimplemented

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway suffers from two broken reactions. rxn_015 incorrectly converts arginine to a simpler intermediate (see P7 feedback). rxn_018 claims to form arginine from mol_022 + glutamic acid via peptide bond formation on montmorillonite, but: (1) peptide bond formation between these molecules would not produce arginine, (2) the description says 'peptide bond formation FROM arginine' not TO arginine, and (3) the molecular formulas don't balance. The initial ornithine guanylation (rxn_013) is sound, but the subsequent steps are chemically incoherent.
suggestions: Remove or completely redesign rxn_015 and rxn_018
If the goal is peptide incorporation, have rxn_015 simply output concentrated arginine, and rxn_018 form arginine-containing dipeptides

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Critically flawed by rxn_016. The reaction is described as 'Reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine on iron oxyhydroxide' but the actual reactants are formate (C1) + NH3 → glutamic acid (C5). This is chemically impossible in a single step — you cannot form a C5 compound from C1 formate without additional carbon sources. The description itself is internally inconsistent (says pyruvate→alanine but the substrate/product pair is formate→glutamic acid). The Barge et al. 2019 reference supports pyruvate→alanine, not formate→glutamic acid. The rest of the cyanosulfidic pathway downstream is sound.
suggestions: Completely redesign rxn_016 — either implement the actual Barge et al. pyruvate→alanine reaction or add proper C1→C5 chain elongation steps via formose-like chemistry
Add intermediate steps for carbon chain building from formate to pyruvate to alpha-ketoglutarate to glutamic acid

pathway_evaluations.feedback: A creative dual-source convergence: HCN polymer hydrolysis for ornithine (rxn_014) plus UV-driven guanidine/cyanamide from ammonium cyanide (rxn_005). The Lohrmann 1972 guanidine synthesis is well-referenced. However, ornithine from HCN polymer is weakly documented (see P5 feedback), and the pathway from guanidine to cyanamide is not explicitly shown (rxn_005 produces both ammonium cyanide and guanidine but rxn_013 needs cyanamide). There's a gap in the logic — guanidine is not the same as cyanamide.
suggestions: Clarify whether rxn_005 produces cyanamide or guanidine — they serve different roles
Add a step converting guanidine to the reactive guanylating agent, or use cyanamide directly

evaluation.network_feedback: This is a thoughtful and well-researched synthesis network that faithfully implements the two most promising prebiotic arginine routes from the literature: the Sutherland cyanosulfidic pathway (Patel et al. 2015) and the ornithine-cyanamide guanylation (Schulze & Winterstein 1899). The network's strengths are substantial: (1) excellent literature alignment — the strategy section honestly acknowledges that no continuous experiment exists, matching the deep research findings precisely; (2) strong core pathways (P1-P4, P6) with individually well-documented reactions; (3) good topology with 5 hub molecules and convergent routes; (4) proper environmental flow from hydrothermal to surface to biochemical in the best pathways. However, three peripheral reactions are seriously flawed: rxn_016 (impossible C1→C5 transformation with mismatched description), rxn_015 (arginine → simpler molecule labeled as 'protection'), and rxn_018 (chemically incoherent peptide formation). These broken reactions drag down P7-P9 but do not undermine the core pathways. The network correctly identifies HCN and cyanamide as central hub molecules and appropriately uses mineral catalysts (greigite, Cu photocatalysts, montmorillonite) at each environmental stage. The 23 molecules and 18 reactions provide adequate complexity without overreach. Overall, this represents the state of the art for prebiotic arginine synthesis planning, with the honest caveat that arginine remains one of the most challenging targets in origin-of-life chemistry.
